item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh, I just found out that the page has been updated, which means 4021 needs to be retranslated.

item.title: Article Reborn
item.content:encoded:
Noting that all messages before this were referring to the old version of the article.

I finally finished my revision of this article, taking a few weeks to write in total. I hope this is a satisfying change for everyone.

I appreciate any feedback or nitpicks you give and I hope you enjoy the new article!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Cant vote either way here. It has serious potential, but the ending has left much to be desired.

item.title: Not bad
item.content:encoded:
I'm a fan. To me it's reminiscent of the skip with the paper dragons. Would be interesting to see if the ethics committee had some sort of bureau in place to prevent the extinction of certain skips. I feel like it could of done more, it tickled my imagination but I feel it needs maybe some more meat to it.

That being said I like where this skip went and I overall still appreciated the concept and execution. +1 from me

Thanks for the contribution!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Is missing the navigation block. as i do not know how to make a navigation block, i cannot add one to the bottom, but thought i should let the poster / the staff know.

item.title: Thank you.
item.content:encoded:
Apparently oranges on legs, like pandas, don't do well in captivity. Poor little oranges. +1

I loved this. What makes it, I think, is the way you go into lifelike technical detail about the care and feeding of a bunch of satsumas on legs. By the way, did you mean them to be that tall? Reading the description, I'm picturing a normal-sized mandarin orange on seven-foot-long spindly legs. Is that right?

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: Noting that the image comes from here and is cc-compliant.

item.title: Flawed and yet...
item.content:encoded:
I don't know why but I find my self drawn to this SCP, which has prevented me from straight up down voting it, however I do still have some problems with this SCP:

+ Issues and Critique
- And Possible Soloutions
  • I see what you trying to do by showing that how the Foundation does not know how to properly contain this SCP, and while it doesn't come off as lolfoundation, it still feels a bit too obvious for most people that their doing something wrong, and can come of as overly incompetent (especially for a foundation that specialises in the containment of the anomalous), which ruins the immersion of the reader.
    • The foundation should begin to recognise a decreasing population quickly, and perhaps dedicate some test logs in which they try to find out why and how the population is decreasing. It doesn't matter if they succeed or not, but the chance that they could save them is all that is needed to keep the narrative engaging, speaking of which…
  • The description focuses a bit too much on the oranges and can make it difficult to transition to the the main narrative (which I'm assuming is related to the extinction of SCP-4021). I get the feeling you were perhaps trying to get the reader to become emotionally attached which can be an effective tool for keeping them engaged, however it slows down the pace and can make the transition feel rough
    • Shorten the description a bit and put more focus on the part in which the SCP is slowly dying off.

In short, this SCP has potential, and despite its flaws I grew still fond of these mandarins, but please address these issues since I do not think many people will be as kind. Novote for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Critted this over IRC as a concept and draft, and the posted version doesn't do much to address most of what my concerns were — that is, that the buildup is too long for what your actual narrative (the extinction of the species) is. The testing log removal doesn't help for me — it still feels too sudden because all the narrative comes flooding within the four notes even with the edits you've made to work it into the description. The removal of the testing log cut down on the buildup, but also loses a lot of the culture you've developed behind the oranges. Ideally I would've liked to see the narrative worked into the log somehow, fleshing out their culture and moving the narrative at the same time.

As it stands, novote from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Porting my forum crit over:

Right from the start, some of the wording could use some changing for better flow/straightforward reading. For example:

+ show block
– hide block
  • SCP-XXXX-1 is to be misted for one hour daily with fresh, distilled water, and requires at least 7 hours per day of either artificial or natural sunlight. > "SCP-XXXX-1 requires at least 7 hours of artificial or natural sunlight per day, and is to be misted daily with fresh distilled water for one hour."
  • The classification of SCP-XXXX is applicable to two different entities: > "SCP-XXXX is the collective designation for the following anomalies:"

Also some general formatting/nitpicks:

  • Personnel Level 1 or higher > "Personnel with Level-1 clearance or higher"
  • Plant seeds are to be provided to SCP-XXXX-2 in order to keep instances content. > what kind of plant seeds? Just any plant seeds? Fruit-specific? Fertile seeds? Any kind of seeds? I personally feel like the Foundation would just keep a designated cache for this anomaly.
  • similarly to ones found in humans > I would use "those" and not "ones"
  • "The average height for an SCP-XXXX-2 instance is 2.34 meters tall." > no need for "tall" since you already mention it's height being measured.
  • SCP-XXXX-2 instances are gradually produced from SCP-XXXX-1. SCP-XXXX-1 appears to self-regulate the amount of SCP-XXXX-2 instances, maintaining a population that does not exceed 17 at any given time. > you can combine these sentences.
  • One (1) packet > Generally, the number (#) numeration is used for extreme precision measurements that can be fatal if misinterpreted, like drug prescriptions. Alternatively, it's for legal reasons to avoid ambiguity. You don't need to do that with easy to count whole numbers.

Overall, I felt like at the end of the article I found myself asking, "so what?" These are little orange entities that do typical human things, but the list of behaviors they exhibit get a little predictable after awhile (the table of reactions seem to amount to "they like this" and "they don't like this", most of which the audience could probably figure out themselves if asked) and the ending of "suddenly they're sad and dying out" feels abrupt. It almost feels kind of underhanded that the ending is just the little orange dudes suddenly starting to do poorly, especially since there isn't really any buildup to why they're doing badly (except hanging out near the entrance? Why didn't the Foundation react accordingly and move them to an open-air aviary or something? (Although, I'm surprised that the Foundation moved the tree to a greenhouse instead of containing it where it was found. Transplanting fruit trees can be tricky business.)

My suggestions:

  • develop the "culture" of these orange entities more. Make them more distinct from humans somehow. Maybe they pray by releasing citric acid scents into the air. Maybe they domesticate bugs.
  • add some more background as to what these things actually are in the context of the Foundation universe. Spirits of farmers who worked in orange orchards? The helpers of some citrus fruit deity, like the Green Warden's mushroom buddies? Something else entirely?
  • don't end the piece on a hamfisted sad note. Give the audience a bit of hope so they don't feel like the story was a letdown. Have the Foundation figure out what's going wrong, change the containment accordingly, and have the last note be about the Foundation stating that the population recovery is a tentative success.

Novote from me since I believe this could be much better, but I don't necessarily think it's downvote-worthy as it is.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So, this one is fairly tame. Anthropomorphic little oranges that long to be free. I actually did enjoy reading about the little buggers, and a part of me felt for their plight. Granted, it takes a while to get to some place substantial, but I think it eventually got there.

All in all, I enjoyed it. So have a +1.

item.title: Site Criticism Team - Review
item.content:encoded:
Noting for transparency that the most I did for this in the draft stages was find a license compliant image (source is here, by the way) and some minor help with questions brought up in the IRC chatrooms. Overall, this did not do much to impress.

+[Critique] Click to Access
-[Click to Hide Text]

This doesn't really go to a place that I find satisfying. One could very much reach the implication that the oranges are dying while in containment, but as for why that is or why the Foundation doesn't pick up on it isn't clear. It almost comes off as incompetent, but not quite.

My primary issue with the article is that a lot of time is spent detailing the behavior of the oranges and their traits. I think that they pray for the dead is very interesting. I think that the tree secretes glucose is a nice touch. However, these things can easily be omitted as they don't support the narrative of the article. Similarly, the table of tests isn't doing much to carry the article either, as it's stuff that could easily be summarized in the description, in my opinion (I am wondering how the Foundation had the foresight to include a metal cage in one of the tests if their intent was to test for the -2's response to live fauna). The weirdness is incredibly tame and not nearly enough.

The build up to the ending does not feel sufficient. I'd even go as far to say that the pacing is a flat line throughout, not really spiking in tension or dread. For me, this didn't feel like it went anywhere.

Disclaimer: Opinions expressed in this review do not reflect the opinions of SCP Wiki staff as a whole. Please respond to RockTeethMothEyes or contact the captain of the Site Criticism Team, SoullessSingularity, for any questions or concerns relating to this review.

item.title: Creation of this SCP
item.content:encoded:
Whew, it's finally out! I had a blast creating this one. This one's my first SCP, and I'm very proud of it! I love the idea of even the foundation making mistakes without knowing, and I decided to incorporate it into this article.

Special thanks to: MalyceGraves, Dyslexion, RockTeethMothEyes, and many others who supported me and gave me great criticism in the IRC chat.

I hope you enjoy this!
